PRODUCT: Remtiza
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Trimetazidine HCl is a medication used to treat angina pectoris.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is classified as an <b>anti-ischemic</b> or <b>metabolic agent</b>, meaning it works by improving the energy efficiency of the heart muscle rather than by directly affecting heart rate or blood pressure.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> The "HCl" in its name denotes the hydrochloride salt form, which is used for stability and solubility.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Trimetazidine is primarily indicated for the symptomatic treatment of <b>stable angina pectoris</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is typically used:<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>As an add-on therapy</b> for patients with stable angina who are not adequately controlled by or are intolerant to first-line anti-anginal therapies like beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>To improve exercise capacity</b> and reduce the frequency of angina attacks.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Trimetazidine's mechanism of action is unique because it modifies the metabolism of the heart muscle cells to make them more efficient at producing energy, particularly under ischemic conditions (when oxygen supply is limited).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Partial Fatty Acid Oxidation Inhibitor:</b> Trimetazidine inhibits an enzyme called <b>3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase</b>, which is involved in the final step of fatty acid oxidation.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> In a healthy heart, fatty acids are the primary source of energy, but they require more oxygen to be metabolized than glucose.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Shifts Energy Metabolism:</b> By partially blocking the oxidation of fatty acids, trimetazidine forces the heart muscle to rely more on <b>glucose oxidation</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Glucose metabolism is more efficient and requires less oxygen to produce the same amount of ATP (the energy currency of the cell).<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Improved Myocardial Efficiency:</b> This metabolic shift from fatty acid oxidation to glucose oxidation results in a more efficient use of the limited oxygen available to the heart muscle during an angina attack.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This helps to maintain the energy levels of the heart cells, preventing the build-up of harmful byproducts and improving the heart's ability to function under stress.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>No Hemodynamic Effects:</b> A key advantage of trimetazidine is that it provides anti-anginal effects without significantly affecting key hemodynamic parameters like heart rate, blood pressure, or cardiac contractility.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This makes it a suitable option for patients who cannot tolerate the side effects of other anti-anginal drugs.</span></span></span></li>
</ol>
-------------------------------------------

PRODUCT: Vildrozon-M
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination of Vildagliptin + Metformin HCl is a single-tablet medication used to treat type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It combines two oral antidiabetic drugs with different but complementary mechanisms of action.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> <b>Vildagliptin</b> is a dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor, and <b>Metformin HCl</b> is a biguanide.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This combination provides a more comprehensive approach to blood sugar control than either drug alone.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This combination therapy is primarily indicated for the treatment of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used in adults who have not achieved adequate glycemic control with Metformin monotherapy or who are already being treated with both vildagliptin and metformin as separate tablets.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This combination is also used as an initial therapy for patients who are likely to require a dual approach to manage their blood sugar effectively.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination works by targeting two different aspects of glucose metabolism:</span></span></p>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">1. Vildagliptin (DPP-4 Inhibitor)<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Vildagliptin works on the <b>incretin system</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It selectively inhibits the enzyme <b>dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This enzyme is responsible for breaking down a group of hormones called <b>incretins</b> (GLP-1 and GIP).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Effect:</b> By preventing their breakdown, vildagliptin increases the levels of active incretins in the body.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> These hormones then stimulate the pancreas to release <b>insulin</b> in a glucose-dependent manner (only when blood sugar is high) and suppress the release of <b>glucagon</b>, a hormone that increases blood sugar.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This helps to control blood glucose levels after meals without a significant risk of hypoglycemia.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">2. Metformin HCl (Biguanide)<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Metformin works primarily in the liver, where it reduces the production of glucose.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It does so by activating the enzyme <b>AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)</b>.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Effect:</b> The activation of AMPK leads to several glucose-lowering effects:</span></span></span>
	<ul style="list-style-type:circle">
		<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Decreased Hepatic Glucose Production:</b> It significantly reduces the liver's production of glucose.</span></span></span></li>
		<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Insulin Sensitivity:</b> It increases the sensitivity of muscle and fat cells to insulin, which improves the uptake of glucose from the bloodstream.</span></span></span></li>
		<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduced Intestinal Glucose Absorption:</b> It also reduces the absorption of glucose from the intestines.</span></span></span></li>
	</ul>
	</li>
</ul>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">Combined Effect</span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination of Vildagliptin and Metformin provides a synergistic effect that addresses multiple aspects of type 2 diabetes:<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Metformin</b> tackles insulin resistance and reduces glucose production by the liver.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Vildagliptin</b> enhances the body's natural ability to regulate blood sugar, particularly after meals, by boosting the incretin system.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This dual action leads to a powerful and comprehensive reduction in both fasting and post-meal blood glucose levels, improving overall glycemic control.</span></span></p>

<p style="margin-bottom:11px">&nbsp;</p>

PRODUCT: Zegwin
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Sitagliptin is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It belongs to the class of drugs called <b>dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Sitagliptin works by enhancing the body's natural ability to control blood sugar levels by regulating the function of the incretin hormones.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<div align="center" style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><hr< source-inline-chip=""></hr<></span></span></span></div>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Sitagliptin is used to improve glycemic control in adults with <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is typically prescribed:<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>As a monotherapy</b> in patients for whom diet and exercise alone do not provide adequate blood sugar control.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>In combination with other oral anti-diabetic medications</b>, such as metformin, a sulfonylurea, or a thiazolidinedione, when a single agent does not provide adequate control.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>In combination with insulin</b> (with or without metformin) when insulin therapy alone does not provide adequate control.</span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<div align="center" style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:11px">
<hr align="center" size="3" width="100%" /></div>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Sitagliptin's mechanism of action is focused on the <b>incretin system</b>, which is a hormonal pathway that helps regulate blood sugar in response to meals.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>DPP-4 Inhibition:</b> Sitagliptin selectively inhibits the enzyme <b>dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This enzyme is responsible for the rapid breakdown and inactivation of a group of hormones called <b>incretins</b>, specifically <b>glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1)</b> and <b>glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Incretin Levels:</b> By inhibiting DPP-4, sitagliptin prevents the rapid degradation of these incretin hormones.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This leads to higher and more prolonged levels of active GLP-1 and GIP in the bloodstream.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Glucose-Dependent Insulin Release:</b> The increased levels of active incretins stimulate the beta cells in the pancreas to release <b>insulin</b> in a glucose-dependent manner. This means that insulin is released primarily when blood sugar levels are high, which helps to control post-meal glucose spikes.</span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduced Glucagon Secretion:</b> The higher incretin levels also suppress the secretion of <b>glucagon</b> from the alpha cells of the pancreas.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Glucagon is a hormone that raises blood sugar by signaling the liver to produce and release glucose.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> By reducing glucagon levels, sitagliptin helps to decrease the liver's glucose output, particularly after a meal.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">In essence, sitagliptin enhances the body's natural response to food, helping to manage blood sugar without a high risk of causing hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), as its effects are dependent on the presence of high glucose levels.</span></span></p>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination of Sitagliptin + Metformin HCl is a single-tablet medication used to treat type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It combines two oral antidiabetic drugs with different but complementary mechanisms of action.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> <b>Sitagliptin</b> is a dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor, and <b>Metformin HCl</b> is a biguanide.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This combination provides a more comprehensive approach to blood sugar control than either drug alone.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This combination therapy is primarily indicated for the treatment of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used in adults who have not achieved adequate glycemic control with Metformin monotherapy or who are already being treated with both sitagliptin and metformin as separate tablets.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This combination is also used as an initial therapy for patients who are likely to require a dual approach to manage their blood sugar effectively.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

Sitagliptin (DPP-4 Inhibitor)</span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Sitagliptin works on the <b>incretin system</b>. It selectively inhibits the enzyme <b>dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This enzyme is responsible for breaking down a group of hormones called <b>incretins</b> (GLP-1 and GIP).<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">2. Metformin HCl (Biguanide)<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Metformin works primarily in the liver, where it reduces the production of glucose. 		<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Insulin Sensitivity:</b> It increases the sensitivity of muscle and fat cells to insulin, which improves the uptake of glucose from the bloodstream.</span></span></span></li>

</ul>

	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Sitagliptin</b> enhances the body's natural ability to regulate blood sugar, particularly after meals, by boosting the incretin system.</span></span></span></li>

HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Empagliflozin is an oral medication used to treat type 2 diabetes and heart failure.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It belongs to a class of drugs known as <b>sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It works by a unique, insulin-independent mechanism to remove excess glucose from the body.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<div align="center" style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:11px">&nbsp;</div>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Empagliflozin is indicated for:</span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus:</b> To improve blood sugar control in adults, either alone or in combination with other anti-diabetic medications.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Cardiovascular Risk Reduction:</b> In adults with type 2 diabetes and established cardiovascular disease, it is used to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Heart Failure:</b> It is indicated to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death and hospitalization for heart failure in adults with a specific type of heart failure, regardless of whether they have diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Chronic Kidney Disease:</b> It is also indicated to reduce the risk of progression of chronic kidney disease in specific patient populations.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<div align="center" style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:11px">&nbsp;</div>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Empagliflozin's mechanism is centered on the kidneys, where it targets the SGLT2 protein.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>SGLT2 Inhibition:</b> The <b>sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2)</b> is a protein located in the proximal convoluted tubule of the kidneys.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Its primary function is to reabsorb about 90% of the glucose that is filtered from the blood back into the bloodstream.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Empagliflozin selectively and reversibly inhibits this protein.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Glucose Excretion:</b> By blocking the SGLT2 transporter, empagliflozin prevents the reabsorption of glucose from the kidney tubules.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This leads to a significant increase in the amount of glucose excreted in the urine, a process called <b>glucosuria</b>.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Blood Glucose Reduction:</b> The loss of glucose through the urine directly lowers blood glucose levels. This effect is independent of insulin, which means it can be effective even in patients with insulin resistance.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="4">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Additional Benefits (for Heart Failure and Kidney Disease):</b> The SGLT2 inhibition also leads to a modest increase in sodium and water excretion (osmotic diuresis). This reduces blood volume and blood pressure, which in turn reduces the workload on the heart and kidneys. This effect is a key reason for its benefits in heart failure and chronic kidney disease, as it reduces cardiac and renal strain.</span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<h3>&nbsp;</h3>
-------------------------------------------

PRODUCT: Empa Plus
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Empagliflozin + Metformin is a single-tablet combination medication used to treat type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It pairs <b>Empagliflozin</b>, a sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitor, with <b>Metformin</b>, a biguanide.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This combination provides a powerful and complementary dual-action approach to blood sugar control by targeting different and essential pathways.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This combination therapy is primarily indicated for the treatment of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used in adults who have not achieved adequate glycemic control with metformin alone or who are already being treated with both empagliflozin and metformin as separate tablets.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">In addition to its glucose-lowering effects, this combination provides the cardiovascular and renal benefits associated with empagliflozin, making it a valuable option for patients who need to reduce their risk of heart disease and kidney issues.</span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination works by targeting two different aspects of glucose metabolism and excretion.</span></span></p>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">1. Empagliflozin (SGLT2 Inhibitor)<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Empagliflozin acts on the kidneys.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It selectively inhibits the <b>sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2)</b>, a protein in the renal tubules responsible for reabsorbing glucose from the urine back into the bloodstream.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Effect:</b> By blocking SGLT2, empagliflozin prevents glucose reabsorption, leading to an increase in glucose excretion in the urine.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This process is insulin-independent, meaning it works regardless of a patient's insulin sensitivity.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">2. Metformin (Biguanide)<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Action:</b> Metformin works primarily in the liver, where it reduces the production of glucose.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It does so by activating the enzyme <b>AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Effect:</b> The activation of AMPK leads to several glucose-lowering effects:</span></span></span>

	<ul style="list-style-type:circle">
		<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Decreased Hepatic Glucose Production:</b> It significantly reduces the liver's production of glucose.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
	</ul>
	</li>
</ul>

<ul style="">
	<li style="list-style-type:none">
	<ul style="list-style-type:circle">
		<li style="margin-left:16px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Insulin Sensitivity:</b> It increases the sensitivity of muscle and fat cells to insulin, which improves the uptake of glucose from the bloodstream.</span></span></span></li>
		<li style="margin-left:16px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list 1.0in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduced Intestinal Glucose Absorption:</b> It also reduces the absorption of glucose from the intestines.</span></span></span></li>
	</ul>
	</li>
</ul>

<h4 style="margin-top:3px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:107%"><span style="font-family:&quot;Calibri Light&quot;,sans-serif"><span style="color:#2e74b5"><span style="font-weight:normal"><span style="font-style:italic">Combined Effect</span></span></span></span></span></span></h4>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The combination of Empagliflozin and Metformin provides a synergistic effect:</span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Metformin</b> tackles insulin resistance and reduces the liver's glucose production, addressing a core issue of type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Empagliflozin</b> removes excess glucose from the body via the kidneys, providing an insulin-independent way to lower blood sugar.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">This dual action leads to a powerful and comprehensive reduction in both fasting and post-meal blood glucose levels, improving overall glycemic control while also providing the added benefits of weight loss and cardiovascular protection.</span></span></p>

<h3>&nbsp;</h3>

<h3>&nbsp;</h3>

PRODUCT: Benglucol
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Metformin HCl is an oral medication widely used to treat type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It belongs to the class of drugs known as <b>biguanides</b>. It is considered a cornerstone of type 2 diabetes management, particularly for overweight patients, due to its effectiveness and favorable safety profile. The "HCl" in its name stands for hydrochloride, a salt form used to make the drug stable and soluble for oral administration.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Metformin HCl is primarily indicated for:</span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus:</b> It is a first-line therapy, either alone or in combination with other oral antidiabetic agents or insulin, to improve blood sugar control.</span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Insulin Resistance:</b> It is often used to manage conditions associated with insulin resistance, such as polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Metformin's mechanism of action is multifaceted, but its primary effect is to decrease glucose production by the liver and increase the sensitivity of cells to insulin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It works by activating the enzyme <b>AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)</b>.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduced Hepatic Glucose Production:</b> Metformin's primary action is to reduce the amount of glucose the liver produces and releases into the bloodstream. It does this by inhibiting gluconeogenesis (the creation of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources) and glycogenolysis (the breakdown of glycogen into glucose).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Insulin Sensitivity:</b> Metformin enhances the sensitivity of muscle and fat cells to insulin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This improves the uptake and utilization of glucose from the blood, thereby lowering blood sugar levels.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduced Intestinal Glucose Absorption:</b> It also has a minor effect on the intestines, where it reduces the absorption of glucose from the food you eat.</span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Unlike some other diabetic medications, metformin does not directly stimulate the pancreas to produce insulin, so it has a very low risk of causing hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) when used as monotherapy. It also has a beneficial effect on lipid profiles and can lead to modest weight loss, making it a highly valued drug for the management of type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<h3>&nbsp;</h3>

PRODUCT: GlipiZIDE-5 Tablet
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glipizide is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It belongs to the class of drugs known as <b>sulfonylureas</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It works by directly stimulating the pancreas to produce and release insulin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glipizide is primarily indicated for the management of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve blood glucose control in adults with type 2 diabetes.</span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glipizide's mechanism of action is focused on the pancreas and is independent of the body's insulin sensitivity.</span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Pancreatic Beta-Cell Stimulation:</b> Glipizide binds to a specific receptor on the surface of pancreatic beta-cells.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This receptor is part of an <b>ATP-sensitive potassium (KATP​) channel</b>.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Channel Closure:</b> The binding of glipizide to this receptor closes the potassium channel.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Membrane Depolarization:</b> The closure of the potassium channel prevents potassium ions from flowing out of the cell, which leads to a buildup of positive charge inside the cell.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This causes the cell membrane to <b>depolarize</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="4">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Calcium Influx and Insulin Release:</b> The depolarization opens nearby voltage-gated calcium channels. Calcium ions then rush into the beta-cell. The influx of calcium is the signal that triggers the fusion of insulin-containing vesicles with the cell membrane, leading to the rapid release of <b>insulin</b> into the bloodstream.</span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">In essence, glipizide works by forcing the pancreas to release more insulin, which in turn lowers blood glucose levels.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This mechanism makes glipizide effective but also carries a risk of <b>hypoglycemia</b> (low blood sugar), as the insulin release is not dependent on current glucose levels.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

PRODUCT: Kelfomet
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <p style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><b><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Description</span></span></b></span></span></span></p>

<p style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glimepiride is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes. It belongs to the class of drugs known as <b>sulfonylureas</b>, which work by stimulating the pancreas to produce and release insulin. Glimepiride is a "third-generation" sulfonylurea, known for its once-daily dosing and potent glucose-lowering effects.</span></span></span></span></span></p>

<div align="center" style="text-align:center">
<hr align="center" size="3" width="100%" /></div>

<p style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><b><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Indications</span></span></b></span></span></span></p>

<p style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glimepiride is primarily indicated for the management of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>. It is used as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve blood glucose control in adults with type 2 diabetes. It can be used as a monotherapy or in combination with other oral antidiabetic agents, such as metformin, or with insulin.</span></span></span></span></span></p>

This receptor is part of an <b>ATP-sensitive potassium (KATP​) channel</b>.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li>

	<li style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><b><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Membrane Depolarization:</span></span></b><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"> The closure of the potassium channel prevents potassium ions from flowing out of the cell, which leads to a buildup of positive charge inside the cell. This causes the cell membrane to <b>depolarize</b>.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><b><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Calcium Influx and Insulin Release:</span></span></b><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"> The depolarization opens nearby voltage-gated calcium channels. Calcium ions then rush into the beta-cell. The influx of calcium is the signal that triggers the fusion of insulin-containing vesicles with the cell membrane, leading to the rapid release of <b>insulin</b> into the bloodstream.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<p style="margin-bottom:11px"><span style="font-size:11pt"><span style="line-height:normal"><span style="font-family:Calibri,sans-serif"><span style="font-size:12.0pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Glimepiride also has some minor extrapancreatic effects, such as increasing insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues, but its primary glucose-lowering effect comes from stimulating insulin release. Like other sulfonylureas, its mechanism of action carries a risk of <b>hypoglycemia</b> (low blood sugar), as the insulin release is not directly dependent on current blood glucose levels.</span></span></span></span></span></p>

PRODUCT: Neglino
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Nateglinide is an oral medication for type 2 diabetes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It belongs to the class of drugs known as <b>meglitinides</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is a short-acting agent that works by stimulating the pancreas to release insulin, particularly in response to meals.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This rapid onset and short duration of action make it effective at controlling post-meal blood sugar spikes.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Nateglinide is primarily indicated for the management of <b>type 2 diabetes mellitus</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve blood glucose control in adults.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is often used in a combination with other oral antidiabetic agents, such as metformin, but is not typically used as monotherapy.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c1279182064="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c1279182064="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c641761624="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Nateglinide's mechanism of action is focused on the pancreas and is similar to that of sulfonylureas, but with a faster onset and a shorter duration.</span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Pancreatic Beta-Cell Stimulation:</b> Nateglinide binds to a specific receptor on the surface of pancreatic beta-cells. This receptor is part of an <b>ATP-sensitive potassium (</b><source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote><b>KATP</b><b>​</b><b>) channel</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Channel Closure:</b> The binding of nateglinide to this receptor closes the potassium channel.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Membrane Depolarization:</b> The closure of the potassium channel prevents potassium ions from flowing out of the cell, which leads to a buildup of positive charge inside the cell. This causes the cell membrane to <b>depolarize</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="4">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Calcium Influx and Insulin Release:</b> The depolarization opens nearby voltage-gated calcium channels. Calcium ions then rush into the beta-cell. The influx of calcium is the signal that triggers the fusion of insulin-containing vesicles with the cell membrane, leading to the rapid release of <b>insulin</b> into the bloodstream.</span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The key difference with nateglinide is its rapid action.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is absorbed quickly and has a short half-life, meaning it works to release insulin during a meal and its effects subside shortly after.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This rapid and transient insulin release helps to manage post-meal glucose excursions (spikes) while reducing the risk of hypoglycemia between meals.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2427743028="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

PRODUCT: V-Ban
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Rivaroxaban, sold under the brand name Xarelto®, is a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is often referred to as a "blood thinner," but it does not actually thin the blood. Instead, it prevents the formation of dangerous blood clots.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is used as a safer alternative to older anticoagulants like warfarin because it has a predictable effect and typically does not require routine blood monitoring.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Rivaroxaban is prescribed to treat and prevent blood clots in various medical conditions.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> The main indications include:<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c448761968="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c448761968="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c3889421858="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Treatment of deep vein thrombosis (DVT)</b> and <b>pulmonary embolism (PE)</b>, as well as the prevention of their recurrence.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Prevention of stroke and systemic embolism</b> in patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation (an irregular heartbeat).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Prophylaxis of DVT</b> in patients who have undergone elective hip or knee replacement surgery.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Reduction of major cardiovascular events</b> (like heart attack or stroke) in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD) or peripheral artery disease (PAD), typically in combination with aspirin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Prevention of venous thromboembolism (VTE)</b> in acutely ill hospitalized patients who are at risk for blood clots.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Treatment of VTE</b> and reduction of recurrent VTE in pediatric patients.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Rivaroxaban works by acting as a <b>direct, selective, and reversible inhibitor of Factor Xa (</b><source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote><b>FXa</b><b>​</b><b>)</b>.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> To understand how this works, you need to know a little about the blood coagulation cascade.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c448761968="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c448761968="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c3889421858="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>The Coagulation Cascade:</b> Blood clotting is a complex process that involves a series of proteins called coagulation factors.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This cascade of reactions ultimately leads to the formation of a stable fibrin clot.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> A key step in this process is the conversion of prothrombin (Factor II) to thrombin (Factor IIa).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>The Role of Factor Xa (FXa​):</b> Factor Xa is a critical enzyme that sits at the junction of the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways of the coagulation cascade.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is responsible for converting prothrombin to thrombin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Rivaroxaban's Action:</b> By directly and competitively inhibiting Factor Xa, rivaroxaban effectively blocks the formation of thrombin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Since thrombin is necessary to convert fibrinogen into the fibrin mesh that forms a clot, its reduction prevents the final step of the coagulation cascade.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This action prevents both free Factor Xa and clot-bound Factor Xa from functioning, thereby stopping the amplification of clot formation.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">A key distinction of rivaroxaban is that it does not require a cofactor like antithrombin III to exert its anticoagulant effect, which is different from older drugs like heparin.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This selective and targeted mechanism of action leads to its predictable effect and makes routine monitoring of blood clotting times, such as INR (International Normalized Ratio), unnecessary for most patients.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></li>
</ol>

<h3>&nbsp;</h3>
-------------------------------------------

PRODUCT: Etamwinslate
HTML_DESCRIPTION: <h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Description</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Ethamsylate is a hemostatic agent, meaning it helps to stop or prevent bleeding.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is a synthetic drug that works by acting on the initial stages of hemostasis, the body's natural process for controlling bleeding.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Unlike other agents that work on the coagulation cascade or fibrinolysis, ethamsylate focuses on improving the function of platelets and capillaries.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is available in oral, injectable, and topical forms.<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c448761968="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c448761968="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c3889421858="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Indications</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">Ethamsylate is primarily used to manage and prevent bleeding from small blood vessels (capillary bleeding).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Its key indications include:<sources-carousel-inline _nghost-ng-c448761968="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"><source-inline-chips _ngcontent-ng-c448761968="" _nghost-ng-c786323692="" class="ng-star-inserted"><source-inline-chip _ngcontent-ng-c786323692="" _nghost-ng-c3889421858="" class="ng-star-inserted"></source-inline-chip></source-inline-chips></sources-carousel-inline></span></span></p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Menorrhagia (Heavy Menstrual Bleeding):</b> It is frequently used to reduce excessive menstrual blood loss.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<p>&nbsp;</p>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Surgical Procedures:</b> It is used to minimize blood loss during and after delicate surgeries, particularly in fields such as ophthalmology, otolaryngology (ear, nose, and throat), gynecology, and dentistry.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Periventricular Hemorrhage:</b> It is used to prevent bleeding into the brain ventricles in premature infants.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<ul>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Other Bleeding Conditions:</b> It can be used for various other conditions involving capillary bleeding, such as hematuria (blood in urine), hematemesis (vomiting blood), or epistaxis (nosebleeds).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ul>

<h3><span style="font-size:13.5pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><span style="font-weight:bold">Mechanism of Action</span></span></span></h3>

<p><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif">The exact and complete mechanism of action of ethamsylate is not fully understood, but it is believed to work through multiple pathways that enhance the initial steps of hemostasis.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Its primary actions are on the capillary walls and platelets:<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></p>

<ol>
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Increased Capillary Resistance:</b> Ethamsylate is thought to strengthen the walls of the capillaries, making them more resistant to rupture.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is believed to do this by restoring the integrity of the endothelial lining and by influencing the synthesis of hyaluronic acid, a component of the extracellular matrix that provides structural support to blood vessels.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="2">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Enhanced Platelet Adhesion and Aggregation:</b> The drug promotes platelet adhesion, the process where platelets stick to a damaged blood vessel wall.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> It is also believed to improve platelet aggregation, which is when platelets clump together to form a primary platelet plug.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> This is thought to involve the promotion of P-selectin-dependent platelet adhesive mechanisms and potentially by modulating prostaglandins that influence platelet function.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>

<ol start="3">
	<li style="margin-left:8px"><span style="font-size:12pt"><span style="tab-stops:list .5in"><span style="font-family:&quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif"><b>Inhibition of Prostaglandins:</b> Ethamsylate inhibits the biosynthesis and action of certain prostaglandins, particularly prostacyclin (PGI2).<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> Prostacyclin is a potent vasodilator and an inhibitor of platelet aggregation.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote> By reducing its effects, ethamsylate creates a more favorable environment for vasoconstriction and platelet aggregation at the site of injury.<source-footnote _nghost-ng-c2077508474="" ng-version="0.0.0-PLACEHOLDER"></source-footnote></span></span></span></li>
</ol>
